4490 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
Fluid/Movable Antenna Meets Active RIS: A Next
Generation ISAC System Design
Jiaju Zhu
, Haisu Wu , Hong Ren , Member, IEEE, Cunhua Pan , Senior Member, IEEE,andRuiShi
Abstract—This paper investigates a ﬂuid antenna system
(FAS)/movable antenna (MA) and active reconﬁgurable intelligent
surface (RIS)-aided integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)
system. Speciﬁcally, we maximize the radar signal-to-noise-ratio
(SNR) by jointly optimizing the beamforming matrix at the base
station (BS), the reﬂecting coefﬁcients of active RIS and port
selection of FAS, subject to the transmit power constraints and
the communication requirements of users. To tackle the problem,
we propose an effective algorithm to decompose the main prob-
lem into three subproblems. For each subproblem, we ﬁrst utilize
semideﬁnite relaxation (SDR), successive convex approximation
(SCA) and penalty function to reformulate the subproblem, then
the majorization-minimization (MM) method is applied to solve the
subproblem iteratively. Numerical results validate the superiority
of the proposed system. In addition, FAS can reduce the resource
consumption and active RIS can overcome the severe path loss
effectively.
Index Terms—Fluid antenna system (FAS), reconﬁgurable
intelligent surface (RIS), active RIS, integrated sensing and
communication (ISAC).
I. INTRODUCTION
I
N THE era of the ﬁfth-generation (5G) and earlier genera-
tions of wireless communication, the systems of communi-
cation and sensing only have limited intersection. However, this
paradigm is expected to shift in future sixth-generation (6G)
network and the emergence of integrated sensing and commu-
nication (ISAC) technique is considered as a convergence point,
bridging the gap of two realms
[1], [2]. As a key technique
of 6G network, the concept of ISAC is not an imagination
and the integration of communication and radar systems is
reciprocal. By designing the signal processing module, hardware
platform and resource allocation, ISAC can not only enhance
spectrum efﬁciency, but also obtain the integration gain and
coordination gain
[1]. Due to these advantages, ISAC can be
Received 25 June 2025; revised 7 October 2025; accepted 21 October 2025.
Date of publication 28 October 2025; date of current version 24 December
2025. This work of Hong Ren was supported in part by the National Natural
Science Foundation of China under Grant 62471138 and Grant 62350710796.
The work of Cunhua Pan was supported in part by the National Natural Science
Foundation of China under Grant 62201137 and Grant 62331023 and in part
by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant
2242025K20001. Recommended for acceptance by Dr. Nan Zhao. (Correspond-
ing author: Hong Ren.)
The authors are with National Mobile Communications Research Lab-
oratory, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China (e-mail: 220250894
@seu.edu.cn; wuhaisu@seu.edu.cn; hren@seu.edu.cn; cpan@seu.edu.cn;
220251225@seu.edu.cn).
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TNSE.2025.3626520
applied in various scenarios including gesture recognition, en-
vironmental imaging and smart home
[1], [2], [3], [4], [5].The
ISAC paradigm can be categorized into two main approaches:
radar-communication coexistence (RCC) and dual-functional
radar and communication (DFRC)
[2]. The former prioritizes
the sharing of spectrum while maintaining separation for the
hardware platform and the transmission signals
[6] while the
latter achieves deeper integration by sharing both hardware
platform and spectrum
[2]. Currently, the combination of DFRC
systems and the new technologies of wireless communication,
such as reconﬁgurable intelligent surface (RIS) and ﬂuid antenna
system (FAS)/movable antenna (MA), has spurred academic
interests signiﬁcantly. As one of the key candidate technologies
for future 6G communications, RIS has attracted increasing
attention due to its hardware architecture
[7], [8], [9], [10],
[11], [12]. RIS is a metamaterial-based planar structure which
comprises various passive reﬂecting elements. Thus, RIS can
enhance the desired signal by intelligently adjusting the phase of
incident signal. Besides, if the user is located in the dead zone of
BS, RIS can construct a virtual line-of-sight (LoS) link between
BS and user. These characteristics of RIS enhance the overall
performance and the coverage range of the signal
[13], [14], [15],
[16], [17], [18]. However, the signals reﬂected by conventional
RIS suffer severe path loss. Recently, a novel architecture of
RIS, termed as active RIS, overcomes the limitations of passive
RIS by incorporating active ampliﬁers into each reﬂecting ele-
ment
[19], [20], [21], [22], [23], [24]. Unlike passive RIS, active
RIS can not only adjust the phase of incident signal, but also
amplify it, which mitigates the “multiplicative fading”. Some
researchers have demonstrated that the active RIS-aided systems
achieve higher achievable rate with the same power budget and
fewer elements
[19], [20]. Besides, the overcome of the severe
path loss enhances the communication and sensing performance
signiﬁcantly
[25], [26], [27], [28], [29], [30].
Conventional MIMO systems usually rely on ﬁxed po-
sition antenna (FPA) arrays, which limits their ability to
utilize the spatial variation of channel characteristics. Re-
cently, a new technique, known as FAS or MA,
1
has received
widespread attention in academic society due to its ability
of adjusting the geometric conﬁguration of antenna and re-
conﬁguring the channel
[31], [32], [33], [34], [35], [36].The
unique feature of FAS enables it to overcome the degrees of
1
For conciseness, we use FAS to represent FAS/MA in the remainder of this
paper.
2327-4697 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies.
Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
ZHU et al.: FLUID/MOVABLE ANTENNA MEETS ACTIVE RIS: A NEXT GENERATION ISAC SYSTEM DESIGN 4491
freedom (DoFs) limitation of FPA and improve the overall
performance
[33].
A. Related Works
1) Researches on Beamforming Design of DFRC Systems:
In DFRC systems, allocating limited resources strategically
to achieve the trade-off between communication and sens-
ing performance has spurred research interests recently
[37].
Some researches focus on the communication performance
while ensuring sensing performance. Authors of
[38] studied
the physical layer security in an ISAC system and minimized
the maximum eavesdropping signal-to-interference-plus-noise-
ratio (SINR) while ensuring the SINR of the targets. In
[39],
the authors considered a full-duplex (FD) ISAC system and
maximized the communication rate while maintaining accurate
sensing performance. In contrast, other works prioritize the sens-
ing performance. For example, the authors of
[40] minimized the
beampattern matching error while satisfying the SINR require-
ments of users. They also demonstrated that the dedicated radar
signal is unnecessary for LoS communication channels. Some
researches further considered the ﬂexible trade-off between
communication and sensing requirement. For instance, in
[41],
the authors proposed a series of optimal transmit beamforming
design of a DFRC system to minimize the weighted multi-user
interference and beampattern matching error. The weighted
sensing mutual information (MI) and the communication total
data rate were maximized in
[42].
2) Researches on RIS-Aided Communication and Sensing:
For ISAC systems, high-frequency signals can enable ultra high
transmit rate and ultra-precise sensing. Nevertheless, their weak
penetration capability renders them highly susceptible to block-
age, degrading the communication and sensing performance
signiﬁcantly. Fortunately, RIS offers a prospective solution for
the aforementioned blockage issue by manipulating the phase
shift and constructing virtual LoS link. In
[18], the authors
considered only the reﬂected link and proposed a joint transmit
and reﬂective beamforming design for a RIS-aided ISAC system
to maximize the minimum beampattern gains. Furthermore,
in
[43], the authors considered both direct and reﬂected links
and maximized the radar MI.
However, as mentioned previously, conventional RIS is com-
posed of passive elements, which causes the signals to suffer
severe path loss, known as the “multiplicative fading” effect.
Speciﬁcally, the path loss of the cascaded link is the product
of those in single links. Thus, deploying the active RIS is an
effective approach to mitigate the path loss. For instance, in
terms of sensing performance, the authors in
[44] investigated
the radar SINR maximization problem in an active RIS-aided
DFRC system with four-hop link. Besides, the authors demon-
strated that the deployment of active RIS can mitigate the “mul-
tiplicative fading” impact. The double active RIS-aided RCC
system was studied in
[45]. Furthermore, for a fundamental
estimation theory perspective, the authors in [46] derived and
minimized the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) and validated the
potential of active RIS to improve estimation performance.
Some researchers studied the physical layer security in active
RIS-aided ISAC systems. For example, the secure rate max-
imization problem was investigated in
[47]. However, in the
considered scenario in
[47], the active RIS only reﬂects the
communication signal. The authors of [48] considered the four-
hop reﬂection and demonstrated the superiority of active RIS in
enhancing the secure data rates and radar signal-to-noise-ratio
(SNR). Nevertheless, the aforementioned ISAC systems all uti-
lize FPA arrays.
3) Researches on FAS-Aided Communication and Sensing:
Existing researches have demonstrated that the deployment of
FAS can enhance the communication performance
[49], [50],
[51], [52], [53], [54]. Building upon the explorations in wireless
communications, some researches have attempted to apply FAS
in ISAC systems. For example, the authors of
[55] and
[56] en-
abled one-dimensional (1D) FAS at the transmitter (Tx) in ISAC
systems. Furthermore, in
[57] and [58], the authors deployed
two-dimensional (2D) FAS in ISAC systems, and numerical
results illustrated that FAS-aided systems achieve better overall
performance than the systems aided by FPA. Moreover, the
authors of
[59] ﬁrst proposed to apply FAS in a RIS-aided ISAC
system. However, the investigation of deploying active RIS in
an FAS-aided ISAC system remains unexplored.
While these studies highlight the growing application poten-
tial of FAS in ISAC, they often rely on an idealized assumption
of continuous antenna positioning. Due to intrinsic hardware
constraints of FAS, discrete position conﬁgurations represent a
more practical paradigm
[60], [61]. Recently, some researchers
have considered discrete scenarios
[62], [63], [64], [65], [66].
For instance, the authors of [62] transformed the discrete po-
sition optimization into the binary programming and utilized
the generalized Bender’s decomposition (GBD) to tackle it.
However, the worst-case complexity of GBD increases exponen-
tially as the number of antennas grows. Moreover, the authors
of
[63] investigated port selection of FAS in an ISAC system.
Nevertheless, the authors considered only 1D scenario.
B. Our Contributions
Based on the above discussion, this paper considers an FAS
and active RIS-aided ISAC system. The differences between
related works and our work were listed in Table
I.Ourmain
contributions are summarized as follows:
1) We consider a FAS and active RIS-aided ISAC system,
where 2D FAS is enabled at the DFRC Tx to fully utilize
the spatial DoFs. Since the direct link between the Tx
and the radar target is obstructed, establishing a virtual
LoS link becomes necessary. Thus, the deployment of the
active RIS can improve the channel characteristics and
enhance both communication and sensing performance.
Then, we formulate the maximization problem of sens-
ing SNR by jointly optimizing the beamforming matrix,
reﬂecting coefﬁcients of active RIS and port selection of
FAS, subject to the communication SINR and transmit
power constraints.
2) In order to tackle the intrinsic non-convexity of the
optimization problem, we propose an alternating
optimization (AO)-based algorithm incorporates
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
4492 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
TAB LE I
COMPARISON BETWEEN RELATED WORKS AND OUR WORK
majorization-minimization (MM), semideﬁnite relaxation
(SDR) and successive convex approximation (SCA)
methods. Speciﬁcally, we optimize the beamforming
matrix by utilizing the SDR method and reﬂecting
coefﬁcients of active RIS by applying the MM method. In
terms of the ports of ﬂuid antennas, we ﬁrst transform the
discrete optimization into binary integer programming,
then adopt the SCA methods and penalty function to
tackle it.
3) Numerical results illustrate the advantages of the FAS and
active RIS-aided ISAC system. Meanwhile, the implemen-
tation of FAS can reduce the resource consumption and
the deployment of active RIS can effectively overcome
the severe path loss.
C. Organizations
This paper is organized as follows: Section
II illustrates the
system model of the FAS and active RIS-aided ISAC system and
formulates the optimization problem. In Section
III, we propose
the AO-based algorithm. Section IV presents the numerical
results and Section
V concludes this paper.
D. Notations
The italics letter x or X, the boldface lowercase letter x and
the boldface uppercase letter X represent values, vectors and
matrices, respectively. (·)
∗
, (·)
T
and (·)
H
denote the conjugate,
transpose and Hermitian operators, respectively. C
M× N
and
R
M× N
represent the set of M × N complex and real matrices.
CN(m, C) denotes that a random variable follows the complex
Gaussian distribution with a mean vector of m and a covariance
matrix of C. E[·] denotes the expectation operation. For a
complex value a, |a| denotes the modulus of a. Besides, Re(a)
and Im(a) denote the real and imaginary part of a, respectively.
Tr(X) and rank(X) denote the trace and the rank of X, respec-
tively. X  0 illustrates that X is a positive semideﬁnite matrix.
X
−1
represents the inverse of X. x
2
denotes the 2-norm of x.
A ⊙ B and A ⊗ B denote the Hadamard product and Kronecker
product of A and B, respectively. diag(X) represents a vector
whose elements are the main diagonal elements of X, and
diag(x) represents a diagonal matrix containing the elements
of x on its main diagonal. vec(X) represents the vectorization
of X. The element in the i-th row and j-th column of X is denoted
as [X]
i,j
.
II. SYSTEM MODEL AND PROBLEM FORMULATION
AsshowninFig.
1, we consider an ISAC system aided by FAS
and active RIS, which comprises a radar target, communication
users, a DFRC Tx and a DFRC receiver (Rx). Speciﬁcally, the
Tx equipped with M ﬂuid antennas serves K users and each
user is equipped with a single antenna. Besides, the active RIS
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

Fig. 1. FAS and active RIS-aided ISAC system.
with N elements is deployed to establish the virtual LoS link
between Tx and target. Moreover, the Rx equipped with L FPAs
is deployed to receive the echo signal.

B. Channel Model
We assume that the moving region of FAS is negligible
compared to the signal propagation distance, satisfying the
far-ﬁeld condition. Thus, the planar wave model is adopted for
the Tx-User and Tx-RIS links
[49]. Moreover, we assume that
only the phases of multi-path channels vary with ﬂuid antenna’s
movement, while the amplitudes, the angle of arrivals (AoAs)
and the angle of departures (AoDs) remain constant.
The FAS-aided system is illustrated in Fig.
1, and the pen-
tagrams represent all available ports. Then, we assume that the
ﬂuid antennas move within a 2D square region.

.
E. Problem Formulation
In this paper, we aim to maximize the radar SNR γ
r
by jointly
optimizing the covariance matrix R of the transmit beamforming
matrix, the reﬂecting coefﬁcients of the active RIS and the port
selection of FAS while satisfying the constraints. Subsequently,
the optimization problem is formulated as
max
R,Ψ,t
γ
r
(18a)
s.t.γ
k
≥ γ
th
, ∀k, (18b)
Tr(R) ≤ P
0
, (18c)
R  0, R
k
 0, ∀k, (18d)
rank(R
k
)=1, ∀k, (18e)
Ψ = diag(ψ
1
, ···,ψ
N
), (18f)
α
n
≤ α
max
, 1 ≤ n ≤ N, (18g)
E

y
H
1
y
1

≤ P
1
, (18h)
t
m
− t
q

2
≥ D
min
,m= q, (18i)
t
m
∈P, 1 ≤ m ≤ M, (18j)
where γ
th
in
(18b) denotes the SINR requirement for com-
munication users. P
0
and P
1
in
(18c) and (18h) denote the
maximum transmit power of the Tx and the RIS, respectively.
The constraint
(18i) ensures that the distance between every two
ﬂuid antennas is greater than or equal to a constant D
min
≥ 0 to
avoid the coupling effect of the antennas.
We notice that the variables R, Ψ and t are coupled intricately,
which poses signiﬁcant challenges to Problem
(18). In addition,
the non-convex constraints
(18b) (18e) (18i) (18j) further com-
plicate the problem.
III. P
ROPOSED ALGORITHM
In this section, in order to tackle the aforementioned chal-
lenges, we employ the AO method to decouple the problem
into three subproblems and solve the subproblems alternatively.
Speciﬁcally, we ﬁrst utilize the SDR method to optimize the
covariance matrix R. Then, the reﬂecting coefﬁcients of the
active RIS Ψ are optimized by the MM method. For the port
selection of FAS, we ﬁrst transform the discrete constraint into
the binary selection matrix. Then, we apply the SCA method to
relax the binary constraint. Finally, we incorporate the relaxed
binary inequality into the objective function as a penalty term.
A. Optimize R
In this subsection, we tackle the optimization of the covariance
matrix R with given t and Ψ.
1) Active RIS Transmission Power Constraint: The con-
straint
(18h) is reformulated as

B. Optimize Ψ
In this subsection, we optimize the reﬂecting coefﬁcients of
the active RIS Ψ without changing the value of R and t.
1) Radar SNR Metric: To overcome the non-concave objec-
tive function γ
r
, we design a lower bound of it by the ﬁrst-order
Taylor expansion shown in Lemma
2.

Problem
(37) is a convex quadratically constrained quadratic
programming (QCQP) problem and can be solved by using
CVX.
C. Optimize t
In this subsection, we select the ports of FAS t when R and
Ψ are ﬁxed.
To begin with, we notice that the key challenge to optimize t
arises from the complexity of channel matrix H(t) and channel
vectors h
H
k
(t). Moreover, the coupling of {t
m
}
M
m=1
introduces
additional non-convexity. Thus, we transform the problem of
port selection into the binary integer programming by reformu-
lating the channel function.

Problem
(60) is a convex problem and can be solved by using
CVX.
D. Convergence and Complexity Analysis
According to above computation and derivations, the overall
algorithm is concluded in Algorithm
1. The proposed algorithm
is based on the AO and MM algorithm. Thus, the solution
to Problem
(18) is continuously increasing in each iteration
of Algorithm
1. Moreover, the solution to Problem (18) has
an upper bound as a consequence of the transmission power
constraint. Thus, the convergence of Algorithm
1 is guaranteed.
Next, we analyze the complexity of the proposed algo-
rithm. Firstly, the approximate complexity of Problem
(22)
is O(K
3.5
M
6.5
ln(1/ǫ))
[70], where ǫ>0 denotes the so-
lution accuracy. Then, the approximate complexity of solv-
ing Problem
(37) is O(t
max

. In addition, the approximate complexity of solv-
ing Problem

. Thus, the complexity of proposed overall
algorithm is O(t

IV. NUMERICAL RESULTS
In this section, numerical results are presented to evaluate
the performance of proposed FAS and active RIS-aided ISAC
system.
A. Parameter Settings
Unless otherwise speciﬁed, the simulation parameters are
deﬁned as follows. As shown in Fig.
2, we assume that the Tx,

the RIS, the target and the Rx are located in (0, 0), (20 m, 10 m),

randomly and uniformly distributed within a circle centered at
(10 m, -20 m) with the radius of 10 meters. For the channel
model, we consider the geometric channel and L

Among which, G
0
represents the path loss at the reference dis-
tance d
0
, d is the propagation distance, and α is the exponent of
path loss. Since the communication users are usually surrounded
by obstructions, we adopt the Rayleigh channel to model the
Tx-User link and the standard deviation of shadow fading is set
to 3dB. Besides, the elevation and azimuth angles for all channel
paths are independent and each of them follows the uniform
distribution across the interval [0,π]. All simulation parameters
are listed in Table
II. Besides, each numerical result is obtained
by averaging over 200 independent channel realizations.
B. Convergence Behaviors
In this subsection, we assess the convergence performance
of the proposed algorithm in some different conditions. As
illustrated in Fig.
3, our algorithm can achieve convergence
within 20 iterations. Besides, the increasing of objective function
is negligible after 10 iterations, which validate the effective con-
vergence behavior of the algorithm. Furthermore, it is observed
that the FAS-aided system exhibits a performance enhancement
of approximately 1 dB compared to the FPA-aided counterpart,
which demonstrates the superiority of enabling FAS in ISAC
Fig. 3. Convergence Behaviors.
Fig. 4. The channel gain map of Tx-RIS link versus the movement of ﬂuid
antennas.
systems. In addition, the number of RIS elements has negligible
impact on convergence behavior.
C. The Relationship Between Channel Gain and FAS
Fig.
4 illustrates a realization of the channel gain for Tx-RIS
link versus the positions of FAS. For a clearer visualization of the
channel gain map, we use a ﬁner grid with Q = 100 in this ﬁgure,
while all other simulations adhere to Q =64as listed in Table
II.
Besides, the channel gain is deﬁned as F(r)
H
Σg(t
0
)
2
2
, where
t
0
represents the coordinates of an antenna. We can observe
that the positions of FAS move from low-gain to high-gain
regions, revealing how FAS fully exploits the spatial variation
of channel characteristics. Moreover, it is noticed that the ﬂuid
antennas do not move to the region with maximum channel
gain, which can be attribute to three main reasons. Firstly, the
discrete positions of FAS restrict the freedom movement of
ﬂuid antennas. Secondly, the ISAC system needs to realize the
trade-off between the communication and sensing performance.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
4500 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
Fig. 5. The beampattern gains in different scenarios.
Lastly, there exist LoS and reﬂecting communication channels
simultaneously. The regions with high-gain in the Tx-RIS link
are likely to have lower gain in the Tx-User link.
D. Beampattern of the Proposed System
Fig.
5 illustrates the beampattern gains at the active RIS for
different conditions. The beampattern gains at the active RIS
can be expressed as
P (θ)=E



a

1
(θ) ΨHx


2

= a
H
1
(θ) ΨHRH
H
Ψ
H
a
1
(θ) , (61)
where θ represents the AoD w.r.t. the active RIS. It is observed
that the FAS-enabled system exhibits 30% enhancement in
beampattern gains at the angle of the target compared to the
FPA-enabled system. Furthermore, increasing the number of
RIS elements not only ampliﬁes the beampattern gains, but also
enhances the spatial resolution by narrowing the major lobe,
which demonstrates the beneﬁt of active RIS in enhancing the
spatial directionality and sensing performance.
E. Performance Comparison With Baseline Schemes
In order to fully assess the performance and demonstrate
the advantages of enabling FAS in an active RIS-aided system,
we compare the radar SNR of our FAS-aided system with the
following three benchmarks:
1) Benchmark 1. Greedy Algorithm (Greedy): In this bench-
mark, we select the ports of antennas sequentially, until
all the M ports are selected.
2) Benchmark 2. FPA: In this benchmark, the Tx deploys an
FPA-based ULA with M antennas spaced at the interval
of 0.5λ.
3) Benchmark 3. Random position antenna (RPA): In this
benchmark, the positions of M antennas are randomly
generated, subject to the minimum distance constraint.
Fig. 6. Radar SNR versus the transmit power at Tx P
0
.
Fig. 7. Radar SNR versus the transmit power at the RIS P
1
.
Fig.
6 presents the relationship between radar SNR and
the transmit power at the Tx. We can observe that the pro-
posed algorithm exhibits approximately 0.5 dB performance
gain compared to the greedy algorithm, which demonstrates the
superiority of our algorithm. Moreover, the proposed algorithm
can achieve the same sensing performance with lower power,
which indicates the beneﬁt of FAS in reducing transmit power.
Furthermore, it is noticed that the growth rate of radar SNR
begins to diminish when P
0
=46dBm. That is reasonable since
the signal power at the active RIS increases with the growth
of P
0
. Thus, the transmit power at RIS is likely to reach its
maximum value P
1
, which constrains the further ampliﬁcation
of reﬂected signal.
The relationship between radar SNR and the transmit power at
the RIS P
1
is presented in Fig.
7. As depicted in Fig. 7, the growth
rate of radar SNR begins to diminish when P
1
=0.3 W, w hi ch
can be attribute to the limitation of maximum ampliﬁcation
factor α
max
. Besides, it is observed that the FAS-aided system
can achieve the same performance with less transmit power at
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
ZHU et al.: FLUID/MOVABLE ANTENNA MEETS ACTIVE RIS: A NEXT GENERATION ISAC SYSTEM DESIGN 4501
Fig. 8. Radar SNR versus the number of channel paths L
p
.
Fig. 9. Radar SNR versus the number of ﬂuid antennas M.
the RIS, which reveals the superiority of FAS in reducing the
power at the active RIS.
Fig.
8 depicts the relationship between the radar SNR and the
number of channel paths. As illustrated in Fig.
8, the increase of
channel paths L
p
results in the deterioration of sensing perfor-
mance. This phenomenon contrasts with prior researches
[49],
[50], [51] where channel capacity improves with channel paths.
The decline of sensing performance can be attributed to the
scattering of the electromagnetic wave
[59], [71].
Fig. 9 depicts the radar SNR of the proposed and benchmark
schemes versus the number of ﬂuid antennas. It is illustrated
that the radar SNR exhibits an enhancement as the number
of antennas M increases. This phenomenon can be ascribed
to the extra DoFs offered by additional antennas. Besides, the
FAS-aided system requires less antennas to achieve the same
performance, which demonstrates the advantage of FAS in re-
ducing the number of antennas.
Fig.
10 presents the impact of the number of available ports
Q on the sensing performance. As expected, the radar SNR
Fig. 10. Radar SNR versus the number of available ports Q.
Fig. 11. CRB for estimating θ
2
.
improves with the number of available ports. This can be at-
tributed to the increment of spatial DoFs. Besides, the growth
rate of sensing performance decreases with the rise of Q, which
indicates that a moderate number of available ports can achieve
adequate performance.
To measure the accuracy of estimating target’s parameters, we
calculate the CRB for estimating θ
2
in Appendix
C and illustrate
the variation of CRB in Fig.
11. The number of transmit symbols
is set as T = 128 to ensure it is large enough. It is observed that
the all schemes reach the minimum value at the expected angle
and FAS can reduce the CRB for estimating the angle of the
target.
F. Performance Comparison With Passive RIS
In this subsection, we demonstrate the superiority of the active
RIS-aided system by comparing the proposed scheme with the
passive RIS scheme. For the fairness of comparison, we consider
the same total power allocation. The power budgets of active and

passive RIS are as follows

act
= P
act

+ P
1

SW
+ P
DC
) ,
P
pas
= P
pas
0

SW
, (62)
where P
act
and P
pas
represent the total power of active and
passive RIS schemes, respectively. Besides, P
act
0
and P
pas
0
de-
note the transmit power at the Tx for active and passive RIS
schemes, respectively. Next, the parameter P
SW
= −5 dBm is
the power utilized for adjusting phase in each RIS element and
P
DW
= −10 dBm represents the biasing power of each active
RIS element
[19]. For the active RIS scheme, the transmit power
of active RIS is set as P
1
=1W and the total power is set as
P
act
=11W. In addition, the remaining power is allocated to
P
act
0
. For the passive RIS scheme, we set the total power as
P
pas
= P
act
=11W and the remaining power is also allocated
to P
pas
0
.
According to aforementioned parameter settings, Fig.
12
illustrates the relationship between sensing performance and
the number of RIS elements. As illustrated in Fig.
12,the
performance of the FAS-aided system with fewer RIS elements
can match that of FPA-aided system. Moreover, the active RIS
schemes signiﬁcantly outperform their passive RIS counterparts
with more RIS elements, which demonstrates that increasing
the number of elements cannot improve the performance sig-
niﬁcantly for passive RIS. This phenomenon also reveals the
advantage of active RIS in overcoming path loss.
V. C
ONCLUSION
This paper investigated a FAS-enabled and active RIS-aided
ISAC system, where the LoS link between Tx and the radar target
is blocked. To improve the overall performance of the ISAC
system, we formulated an optimization problem to maximize
the radar SNR by jointly optimizing the beamforming matrix
at the Tx, the reﬂecting coefﬁcients of active RIS and the port
selection of FAS. Given that the problem is highly non-convex
and the variables are tightly coupled, we proposed an AO-based
algorithm to divide the main problem into three subproblems.
Speciﬁcally, we ﬁrst leveraged the SDR method to optimize
the beamforming matrix, then, the MM method was adopted
to optimize the reﬂecting coefﬁcients of active RIS. For the
port selection of FAS, we ﬁrst transformed the subproblem into
binary programming, then the penalty function and SCA method
were applied to tackle it. Our numerical results illustrated the
superiority of the FAS and active RIS-aided ISAC system, where
the enabling of FAS reduces the resource consumption (e.g.
the number of antennas, the number of RIS elements and the
transmit power) and the assistance of active RIS overcomes se-
vere path loss effectively. In addition, existing researches mainly
focus on sensing single point target. Nevertheless, sensing ex-
tended target or multi targets is a more practical scenario and
its implementation in FAS and active RIS-aided ISAC systems
remains unexplored.
A

EFERENCES

functional wireless networks for 6G and beyond,” IEEE J. Sel. Areas

Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
ZHU et al.: FLUID/MOVABLE ANTENNA MEETS ACTIVE RIS: A NEXT GENERATION ISAC SYSTEM DESIGN 4505
Jiaju Zhu received the B.S. degree from the College
of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin Uni-
versity, Changchun, China, in 2025. He is currently
working toward the M.S. degree with the School of
Information Science and Engineering, Southeast Uni-
versity, Nanjing, China. His research interests include
integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) and
5G NR protocol framework.
Haisu Wu received the B.S. degree in electronic and
information engineering from Shandong University,
Jinan, China, in 2024. He is currently working toward
the M.S. degree with the School of Information Sci-
ence and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing,
China. His research interests include integrated sens-
ing and communications (ISAC) and its application
within 5G NR protocol framework.
Hong Ren (Member, IEEE) received the B.S. degree
from the School of Information Science and Engi-
neering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu,
China, in 2011, and the M.S. and Ph.D. degrees from
Southeast University, Nanjing, China, in 2014 and
2018, respectively. From 2016 to 2018, she was a
Visiting Student with the University of Southampton,
U.K., and was also a Postdoctoral Scholar with the
Queen Mary University of London, London, U.K.,
from 2018 to 2020. She is currently an Associate
Professor with Southeast University. Her research
interests include communication and signal processing, cooperative ISAC, AI,
and URLLC. She is also the Editor of IEEE T
RANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR
TECHNOLOGY and IEEE WIRELESS COMMUNICATION LETTERS. She was recog-
nized as a Clarivate Highly Cited Researcher in 2024. She was the recipient of
the 2024 IEEE Communications Society Fred W. Ellersick Prize and the 2022
IEEE Communications Society Leonard G. Abraham Prize.
Cunhua Pan (Senior Member, IEEE) is currently a
Full Professor with Southeast University, Nanjing,
China. He has authored or coauthored more than
200 IEEE journal papers. His papers got more than
23,000 Google Scholar citations with H-index of 76.
His research interests mainly include reconﬁgurable
intelligent surfaces (RIS), AI for Wireless, near ﬁeld
communications and sensing, and integrated sensing
and communications. He is/was the Editor of IEEE
T
RANSACTION ON COMMUNICATIONS, IEEE TRANS-
ACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, IEEE WIRE-
LESS COMMUNICATION LETTERS, and IEEE COMMUNICATIONS LETTERS.Heis
also the (Leading) Guest Editors of IEEE JOURNAL ON SELECTED AREAS IN
COMMUNICATIONS, IEEE JOURNAL OF SELECTED TOPICS IN SIGNAL PROCESS-
ING, IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS, IEEE Network Magazine, IEEE Vehicular
Technology Magazine, IEEE Internet of Things Magazine, IEEE TRANSACTIONS
ON GREEN COMMUNICATIONS AND NETWORKING. He was the recipient of the
IEEE ComSoc Leonard G. Abraham Prize in 2022, IEEE ComSoc Asia-Paciﬁc
Outstanding Young Researcher Award in 2022, IEEE ComSoc Fred W. Ellersick
Prize in 2024, IEEE ComSoc CTTC Early Achievement Award in 2024, IEEE
ComSoc SPCC Early Achievement Award in 2024, IEEE ComSoc RCC Early
Achievement Award in 2025, and IEEE ComSoc Heinrich Hertz Awardin 2025,
and his supervised Ph.D. thesis was the recipient of the IEEE Signal Processing
Society Best Ph.D Dissertation Award in 2024. He is also a Clarivate Highly
Cited Researcher.
Rui Shi received the B.S. degree from the School of
Communication and Information Engineering, Nan-
jing University of Posts and Telecommunications,
Nanjing, China, in 2025. She is currently working to-
ward the M.S. degree with the School of Information
Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nan-
jing. Her research interests include reconﬁgurable
intelligent surface (RIS) and integrated sensing and
communications (ISAC).
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:00 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
